chunk_summary: This section distinguishes picky eating from pediatric feeding disorder (PFD), emphasizing functional impact and domain-specific dysfunctions. It highlights the importance of targeted interventions and differentiates ARFID from anorexia-spectrum illnesses in older children.
learning_objectives: Understand the distinction between developmentally common picky eating and pediatric feeding disorder (PFD).
Identify the four domains of dysfunction associated with PFD: medical, nutritional, feeding skill, and psychosocial.
Recognize the clinical and functional criteria for diagnosing PFD versus picky eating.
Differentiate ARFID from anorexia-spectrum disorders in older children and adolescents.
key_facts: PFD is defined as persistent impaired oral intake that is not age-appropriate and involves dysfunction in one or more domains: medical, nutritional, feeding skill, and psychosocial.
Picky eating is common and not necessarily pathological unless it impacts growth, nutrition, or family functioning.
In India, inappropriate responses like supplements and coercion can worsen feeding aversion and delay diagnosis of organic disease.
ARFID (Avoidant-Restrictive Food Intake Disorder) is characterized by severe restrictive intake without body-image concerns, unlike anorexia-spectrum illnesses.
ARFID treatment focuses on exposure-based and anxiety-focused approaches, not appetite tonics or weight-shape counselling.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: "PFD provides an operational umbrella for persistent impaired oral intake that is not age-appropriate and is associated with dysfunction in one or more domains: medical, nutritional, feeding skill, and psychosocial." (Reference 1)
document_key_topics: Picky eating versus pediatric feeding disorder (PFD)
Impact of feeding difficulties on growth and family functioning
